content:
First observe that if \(n\) has any digit 0 then its digit‐product is 0, so we would need
\[
n^2-15n-27=0,
\]
which has no integer solutions.  Hence all digits of \(n\) are between 1 and 9, and in particular the digit‐product
\[
P(n)\;\le\;9^d,
\]
where \(d\) is the number of digits of \(n\).

On the other hand we require
\[
P(n)\;=\;n^2-15n-27\;\ge\;0
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
n^2-15n-27\ge0
\;\Longrightarrow\;
n\ge17\quad(\text{since the quadratic vanishes at }n\approx-1.68,\,16.68).
\]

Case 1: \(n\) is a two‐digit number, \(10\le n\le99\).  Then \(P(n)\le9\cdot9=81\), so
\[
n^2-15n-27\;\le\;81
\;\Longrightarrow\;
n^2-15n-108\le0
\;\Longrightarrow\;
-5.32\le n\le20.32.
\]
Together with \(n\ge17\) and \(10\le n\le99\) this forces \(17\le n\le20\).  We check:

\[
\begin{aligned}
n=17&:\;P(n)=1\cdot7=7,\quad17^2-15\cdot17-27=289-255-27=7\quad\checkmark,\\
n=18&:\;P(n)=8,\quad324-270-27=27\;( \ne8),\\
n=19&:\;P(n)=9,\quad361-285-27=49\;( \ne9),\\
n=20&:\;P(n)=0,\quad400-300-27=73\;( \ne0).
\end{aligned}
\]
Thus the only solution in two digits is \(n=17\).

Case 2: \(n\) has 3 or more digits.  A similar “\(n^2\) vs.\ \(9^d\)” bound shows that already for \(n\ge100\),
\[
n^2-15n-27>9^3=729,
\]
and for yet larger \(d\), \(n^2-15n-27\) grows far faster than \(9^d\).  Hence there are no solutions beyond two digits.

Conclusion: the only positive integer solution is \(n=17\), so the sum of all solutions is
\[
\boxed{17}.
\]
